title: Same as Ever
author: Morgan Housel
thesis: Most of life's important truths don't change.
remember: Calibrate to the things that don't change — fear, greed, attention.
changed: I now ask of any prediction: what would it look like in a world that hasn't fundamentally changed?

title: The Innovator's Solution
author: Christensen & Raynor
thesis: Disruption happens from below; incumbents serve their best customers right off the cliff.
remember: The 'good management' that protects Q4 is what kills companies on a 10-year horizon.
changed: Reframed how I read every product roadmap — what is being defended vs what's being built.

title: Bird by Bird
author: Anne Lamott
thesis: Write shitty first drafts. Show up every day. Bird by bird means one small thing at a time.
remember: 'Bird by bird, buddy. Just take it bird by bird.' — her father's line on how to write a school paper about birds.
changed: I stopped trying to write 'the chapter' and started writing the next paragraph.

title: The Power of Habit
author: Charles Duhigg
thesis: Cue → routine → reward, the loop. Identify it to interrupt or design it.
remember: The keystone-habit idea — small habits that pull others up with them.
changed: Rebuilt my morning routine around one keystone (sunlight in the first 30 min).

title: How to Take Smart Notes
author: Sönke Ahrens
thesis: The Zettelkasten method: every note is its own atomic idea, linked to others, written in your own words.
remember: Notes that simply restate aren't notes — they're filing.
changed: I rewrote my notes vault to enforce 'one idea per note' and the writing got better immediately.

title: The Three-Body Problem
author: Liu Cixin
thesis: Civilizations are not static — and not necessarily friendly. The Dark Forest is a chilling but coherent answer to Fermi.
remember: The chilling line about radio silence being the only safe strategy.
changed: Made me less optimistic about cosmic optimism.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Andy Weir
thesis: Friendship can transcend biology, language, and even species — if both parties are problem-solvers in good faith.
changed: Reminded me how rare a book is that makes you feel pure delight cover-to-cover.

title: Breath
author: James Nestor
thesis: How you breathe shapes your physiology more than nearly any other lever.
remember: Mouth-tape your 1-year experiment with mouth tape — sleep got measurably better.
changed: I started nasal breathing during workouts and it changed my zone-2 capacity.

title: The Creative Act
author: Rick Rubin
thesis: Make things to find out what you think. The work is the antenna.
remember: 'Do not seek to be right. Seek to be true.'
changed: Creative blocks usually aren't blocks — they're protections from being seen.

title: Tomorrow and Tomorrow and Tomorrow
author: Gabrielle Zevin
thesis: A novel about love, work, and games that's actually about how creative collaboration breaks people down and remakes them.
remember: The phrase 'tomorrow and tomorrow and tomorrow' as a meditation on work that never quite finishes.
changed: Made me less guarded about how much my work and my closest collaborations bleed into each other.

title: The Coddling of the American Mind
author: Lukianoff & Haidt

title: Range
author: David Epstein
remember: The Tiger vs Federer parable — sampling and switching aren't bugs, they're the path.
changed: I stopped feeling guilty about reading widely instead of deeply in one field.

title: Atomic Habits
author: James Clear
thesis: Small consistent changes compound. Identity-based habits beat outcome-based ones.
remember: 'You don't rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems.'
changed: Switched my habit framing from 'I want to run' to 'I am a runner.'

title: Thinking in Systems
author: Donella Meadows
thesis: Systems thinking: feedback loops, leverage points, and why fixing the symptom moves the problem elsewhere.
remember: The 12 leverage points hierarchy — the highest leverage is changing the goals of the system, not the parameters.
changed: I now look for the goal of any system before trying to optimize within it.

title: Recursion
author: Blake Crouch
thesis: Memory makes reality. Pull on that thread and the universe unravels.
remember: The line about how grief is the price for love and you wouldn't actually want to undo it.
changed: More aware of how a single intense memory can color years of perception afterward.
